elements.sent-2411-001: We discuss the usability of the gravitational wave detector LISA for studying the orientational distribution of compact white dwarf binaries in the Galactic bulge.
elements.sent-2411-002: We pay special attention to measuring the dipole pattern of the distribution around the Galactic rotation axis.
elements.sent-2411-003: Based on our new formulation, which leverages the parity properties of the involved systems, we found that the apparent thickness of the bulge in the sky becomes critical for the dipole measurement.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also discuss the extra-Galactic studies for black hole binaries and neutron star binaries with BBO/DECIGO.
elements.sent-2411-005: Over the past 100 years, many researchers have been interested in whether the orbital orientations of nearby binaries are correlated with Galactic structures (see e.g. \cite{chang for early works).}
elements.sent-2411-006: The astrometric data of visual binaries have been the primary resources in this context.
elements.sent-2411-007: We can find the recent observational situations on nearby binaries in the detailed study by Agati et al. \cite{agati .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Analyzing 95 binaries within 18pc from the Sun, they argued that these binaries appear to be randomly oriented.
elements.sent-2411-009: Meanwhile, in 2023, Tan et al. \cite{tan published a paper on the orientations of the symmetric axes of planetary nebulae (PNe) around the Galactic bulge.}
elements.sent-2411-010: They selected 14 PNe that host (or are inferred to host) short-period ($\lsim 1$ day) binaries.
elements.sent-2411-011: They found that the symmetric axes of these PNe tend to be parallel to the Galactic plane.
elements.sent-2411-012: Since PNe are gases ejected during the formation of white dwarfs \cite{kwi , the symmetric axes of the selected PNe are considered to align with the orbital axes of the inner short-period binaries.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Therefore, the observed patterns of the PNe suggest that the associated 14 short-period binaries around the bulge are not randomly oriented \cite{tan , in contrast to the aforementioned binaries around the Sun \cite{agati .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Binaries are fundamental systems in astrophysics \cite{Duchene:2013cba , and their formation process could be highly diverse even in the Galaxy.}
elements.sent-2411-015: We are encouraged to make further observational studies on orientations of binaries in various environments.
elements.sent-2411-016: For the orbital angular momentum orientation (denoted below by the unit vector $\vec j$) of a Galactic binary, considering the whole-part relation, one would be particularly interested in its possible statistical alignment with the Galactic rotation vector $\vec q$.
elements.sent-2411-017: More specifically, if we take the rotation vector $\vec q$ as the polar direction for the spherical harmonic expansion, the dipole pattern $(l,m)=(1,0)$ of the unit vector $\vec j$ would be a high-priority target.
elements.sent-2411-018: Unfortunately, the symmetric axes of the PNe are insensitive to the odd $l$ patterns, since the observed PN axes do not allow us to distinguish between $\vec j$ and $-\vec j$.
elements.sent-2411-019: We will later comment on this point again, in relation to the parity properties of the spherical harmonics.
elements.sent-2411-020: Note that the two vectors $\vec j$ and $\vec q$ are both axial, and the harmonic expansion does not depend on the adopted handedness.
elements.sent-2411-021: The proposed space gravitational wave (GW) interferometer LISA is expected to detect $\sim 10^4$ close white dwarf binaries (CWDBs) in the Galaxy \cite{LISA:2022yao,Hils:1990vc,Nelemans:2000es,Ruiter:2007xx,Breivik:2019lmt,Nissanke:2012eh,Korol:2017qcx,Lamberts:2019nyk and determine their geometrical configuration \cite{Cutler:1997ta,Takahashi:2002ky .}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A significant fraction (e.g. $\sim 30\%$) of the CWDBs are estimated to be the bulge component \cite{Ruiter:2007xx .}
elements.sent-2411-023: It should also be stressed that, for LISA, we can easily control the selection effects, e.g. by using CWDBs only above $\sim 4$mHz, where LISA will make a complete Galactic survey \cite{LISA:2022yao,Seto:2022iuf (see also \cite{gaia for the selection effects of the Gaia binary survey).}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, as pointed out by the author \cite{Seto:2024odc , LISA could be an essentially new observatory to examine the orientational distribution of Galactic binaries relative to the Galactic structure.}
elements.sent-2411-025: However, in the previous paper \cite{Seto:2024odc , the author applied a distant observer approximation, in which the angular thickness of the bulge was ignored.}
elements.sent-2411-026: In fact, as shown later, this approximation results in a blindness to the odd $l$-patterns, including the dipole mode.
elements.sent-2411-027: In this work, as a follow-on study, we pay a special attention to the dipole pattern measurement, now carefully taking into account the small bulge thickness.
elements.sent-2411-028: In addition, we newly introduce appropriate estimators for simultaneously measuring various harmonic patterns.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our approach can be expanded to extra-Galactic studies for binaries composed by black holes and neutron stars.
elements.sent-2411-030: We will make a brief discussion on the prospect with BBO and DECIGO.
elements.sent-2411-031: II, we discuss the axisymmetric model for the angular momentum distribution of the bulge CWDBs and make its spherical harmonic decomposition.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-032: III, we explain the fourfold degeneracy at estimating the polarization angle for a CWDB with LISA.
elements.sent-2411-033: We apply a systematic scheme for handling the degeneracy.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-034: IV, we introduce simple estimators to separately measure the observable spherical harmonic patterns of the orientational distribution function.
elements.sent-2411-035: We also present the shot noise limits for the dipole and quadrupole measurements.. {In Sec.}
elements.sent-2411-036: V, we estimate the fluctuations induced by the parameter estimation errors.. In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-037: VI, we discuss the extra-Galactic studies with BBO and DECIGO.. Sec.
elements.sent-2411-038: VII is devoted to a short summary.
elements.sent-2411-039: For CWDBs in the Galactic bulge, the joint probability distribution function for the orbital orientations $\vec j$ and the sky directions $\vec n$ can be generally expressed as ${\cal P (\vec j,\vec n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Here, in view of the mixing processes discussed below, we assume that they are independently distributed ${\cal P (\vec j,\vec n)={\cal P (\vec j){\cal R (\vec n)$ and the function ${\cal P (\vec j)$ is axisymmetric around the Galactic rotation axis $\vec q$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Given the probable bar-like structure in the central part of our Galaxy \cite{ga,ga2 , one might conversely expect that there should be strong azimuthal (in particular $m=\pm 2$) patterns in the function ${\cal P ({\vec j )$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: However, the formation epochs of the bulge CWDBs were likely to be more broadly distributed than the rotation period of the bar (currently $\sim 10^8$ yr \cite{ga,ga2 ) \cite{Nelemans:2000es .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Therefore, even if the orientations of the binaries have some azimuthal patterns ($m\ne 0$) at their formation epochs, the chronological phase mixing should largely reduce the azimuthal patterns in the integrated population at present.
elements.sent-2411-044: In contrast, the axisymmetric patterns $m=0$ would be more resistant to the mixing.
elements.sent-2411-045: One could also expect positional intermixture facilitating independence between $\vec j$ and $\vec n$, since the majority of bulge stars have not been corotating with the bar and also have random velocities \cite{ga,ga2,gd .}
elements.sent-2411-046: In the present explanation, for simplicity, we supposed that the orientation vectors $\vec j$ had been frozen since the binary formation, as discussed in \cite{tan .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Below, we mainly use the Galactic latitude $b$ and longitude $\eta$ to represent the binary position $\vec n$ in the sky, and put ${\cal R ({\vec n )={\cal R (b,\eta)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The axisymmetric function ${\cal P (\vec j)$ should depend only on the angle $\gamma$ defined by the product $\cos \gamma={\vec j}\cdot {\vec q}$, thus containing only $m=0$ patterns \beq {\cal P ({\vec j )=\sum_{l=0 ^\infty a_{l0 Y_{l0 (\gamma)=\sum_{l=0 ^\infty\sqrt{\frac{2l+1 {4\pi a_{l0 L_l({\vec j}\cdot {\vec q ) \equiv {\cal P _A(\cos\gamma) \eeq with the normalization condition $a_{00 =1/\sqrt{4\pi}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The spherical harmonics $Y_{lm}$ are generally expressed as \beq Y_{lm (\theta,\phi)=\sqrt{\frac{2l+1 {4\pi}\frac{(l-m)! {(l+m)! L_{lm (\cos\theta)e^{im\phi \eeq with the associated Legendre polynomials $L_{lm}$, including the special cases $L_l\equiv L_{l0}$ for $m=0$ \cite{Jackson:1998nia .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: We present concrete examples for the Legendre polynomials $L_l$ \beq L_0=1,~ L_1(\alpha)=\alpha,~L_2(\alpha)=\frac{(3\alpha^2-1) 2.}
elements.sent-2411-051: From Eq. (\ref{aa10 ), we can easily show the parity relation \beq Y_{lm (-\vec j)=(-1)^l Y_{lm (\vec j).. \eeq.}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{delta-crop.pdf} \caption{Geometrical configuration of a binary (at the Galactic latitude $b$) on the slice of a constant Galactic longitude. [Directional vectors]: All four vectors (${\vec q}, {\vec n}, \vec e_q,\vec e_r)$ are unit vectors. The vector $\vec n$ shows the direction of the binary, and $\vec e_q$ is the transverse projection of the Galactic rotation axis $\vec q$ with the offset angle $b$. We also set $\vec e_r=\vec n\times \vec e_q$. [Source frame]: We define the source frame as the polar coordinate system with the polar direction $-\vec n$ (GW propagation direction) and set the reference direction $\vec e_q$ for the azimuthal angle. In this source frame, the orientation of the binary $\vec j$ (not shown here) is specified by the inclination (polar) angle $I$ and the polarization (azimuthal) angle $\psi$. } \label{fig:del}
elements.sent-2411-052: To discuss GW strain observation (particularly for Sec.
elements.sent-2411-053: III), it is crucially advantageous to work in a polar coordinate system, where polar axis is directed in the GW propagation direction ($-\vec n$ in Fig. 1).
elements.sent-2411-054: In Fig. 1, there is still freedom to set the reference direction for the azimuthal angle of the polar coordinate and use the projected vector $\vec e_q$, respecting the available Galactic symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-055: We hereafter refer to this polar coordinate system as the source frame, and the orientation vector $\vec j$ will be decomposed into the polar angle $I$ and the azimuthal angle $\psi$ (respectively called the inclination and polarization angles).
elements.sent-2411-056: In the source frame, the Galactic axis $\vec q$ is given by $(\pi/2+b,0)$ with the Galactic latitude $ b$.
elements.sent-2411-057: We next evaluate the orientational distribution functions $p(I,\psi|b)$ for the two variables $(I,\psi)$ at a given sky direction $(b,\eta)$ (actually independent of the Galactic longitude $\eta$ due to the symmetry around $\vec q$).
elements.sent-2411-058: We just need to rewrite the function ${\cal P _A(\cos\gamma)$ in Eq. (\ref{pa ) in terms of the variables $(I,\psi)$, taking into account the offset angle $b$.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Here the addition theorem for the spherical harmonics works quite efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-060: For two unit vectors $\vec x$ and $\vec y$, we generally have \cite{Jackson:1998nia \beq L_l({\vec x}\cdot {\vec y )=\frac{4\pi {2l+1}\sum_{m=-l ^lY_{lm (\vec x)Y^*_{lm (\vec y).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: From Eq. (\ref{aa10 ), we can readily obtain the parity relations \beq D_{lm (-b)=(-1)^{l+m D_{lm (b).. \eeq From Eqs.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: For each degree $l$, we can confirm the power conservation \beq \sum_{m=-l ^lD_{lm (b)^2=1 \eeq (e.g. directly putting $\vec x=\vec y=\vec q$ in Eq. (\ref{add ) and using $L_l(1)=1$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We present the explicit form for the dipole coefficient \beq D_{10 (b)=-\sin b.}
elements.sent-2411-064: For $|b|\ll 1$ (valid for our bulge CWDBs), we have $D_{10 (b)=O(b)$ and the most of the power is stored in the $m=\pm1$ modes with \beq |D_{11 (b)|=|D_{1-1 (b)|\simeq 1/\sqrt2.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{ca-crop.pdf} \caption{Dipole patterns $Y_{10}(\gamma)$ in the source frame. The red and blue respectively show positive and negative values. (a) For binaries at a Galactic latitude $b$, the intrinsic dipole pattern $Y_{10}(\gamma)$ is tilted by the angle $b$. (b) As shown in Eq. (\ref{crs}), the coefficient $D_{10}(b)=-\sin b$ is the cross product between the tilted profile $Y_{10}(\gamma)$ and the probe $Y_{10}^*(I)=Y_{10}(I)$ (edged with the yellow line), which is axisymmetric around the polar direction $-\vec n$. From the symmetries, we can easily see $D_{10}(-b)=-D_{10}(b)$ with $D_{10}(0)=0$. (c) Dipole patterns from the pairwise latitudinal directions $\pm b$. (d) When combined in the same phase, the total profile becomes antisymmetric in the directions $\pm \vec e_q$, and the product with the axisymmetric one $Y_{10}(I)$ vanishes with $g_{100}=0$. (e) When combined in the opposite phase, the total profile becomes vertically symmetric, resulting in $g_{101}\ne 0$. } \label{fig:del}
elements.sent-2411-065: LISA will be a drastically new tool to geometrically explore the Galactic CWDBs.
elements.sent-2411-066: However, observationally, we cannot uniquely specify the polarization angle $\psi$ for each bulge CWDB from its GW signal.
elements.sent-2411-067: There are actually four possible solutions (see e.g. \cite{Cornish:2003vj ).}
elements.sent-2411-068: Following the previous paper \cite{Seto:2024odc , we explain the basic aspects of the fourfold degeneracy and apply a simple prescription for our pattern analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Note that most of the CWDBs are expected to have negligible eccentricities, e.g. due to tidal dissipation effects.
elements.sent-2411-070: First, let us consider a circular binary with the orientation angles $(I,\psi)$ and rotate it around the polar axis $-\vec n$ by $180^\circ$.
elements.sent-2411-071: The new binary has the angles $(I,\psi+\pi)$, but its gravitational waveform is identical to that of the original one, because of the spin-2 nature of the wave \cite{pw .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Correspondingly, using only GW data, we cannot determine which of the two values $\psi$ and $\psi+\pi$ is the true polarization angle.
elements.sent-2411-073: In fact, for the CWDBs, there will be more spurious solutions other than $\psi+\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-074: If we rotate the binary now by $90^\circ$ (namely $\psi\to \psi+\pi/2$), the polarization tensors change signs.
elements.sent-2411-075: For the lowest quadrupole signal, these signs can be absorbed as the overall phase shift, resulting in the signal degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-076: We can, in principle, distinguish the two states $(I,\psi)$ and $(I,\psi+\pi/2)$ by using the post Newtonian effects (see e.g. \cite{pw,LIGOScientific:2020stg ).}
elements.sent-2411-077: Unfortunately, these relativistic effects are very weak for our bulge CWDBs in the mHz band and will be totally masked by the measurement noise of LISA.
elements.sent-2411-078: Taking into account the spin-2 character again, there will be fourfold degeneracy between $\psi,\psi+\pi/2,\psi+\pi$ and $\psi+3\pi/2$ (namely three spurious solutions).
elements.sent-2411-079: LISA will newly provide us with orientational information for thousands of bulge CWDBs.
elements.sent-2411-080: At some stage, we need to appropriately address the fourfold degeneracy not just twofold.
elements.sent-2411-081: Here, as proposed in \cite{Seto:2024odc , we identify the four solutions by compactifying the range of the polarization angle $\psi$ from the original one $[0,2\pi]$ to the folded one $[0,\pi/2]$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: More concretely, we consider the following folded function \beqa {\bar p (I,\psi|b) \equiv p(I,\psi|b) +p(I,\psi+\pi/2|b)\nonumber + p(I,\psi+\pi|b) + p(I,\psi+3\pi/2|b) \eeqa as our observable distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-083: This is a systematic approach without ambiguities.
elements.sent-2411-084: Here it is essential to work in the source frame.
elements.sent-2411-085: Importantly, the folding operation has a strong side effect.
elements.sent-2411-086: In Eq. (\ref{fold ), each harmonic term $Y_{lm}$ from Eq. (\ref{pdf132 ) is now proportional to $\sum_{k=0 ^3 \exp(im k\pi/2)$, which vanishes unless the order $m$ is multiple of 4.}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Therefore, we can write down \beq {\bar p (I,\psi|b)=4\sum_{l=0}\sum_{m\in K ^{|m|\le l a_{l0 D_{lm (b)Y_{lm (I,\psi) \eeq with the group \beq K\equiv \lnk 0,\pm4,\pm8,\cdots\rnk.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: As mentioned earlier, in original function (\ref{pdf132 ), our target signal $a_{10}$ is mainly contained in the patterns $Y_{1,\pm1}$ with $|D_{1,\pm1 |\simeq 1/\sqrt2$ for the bulge directions at $|b|\ll 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: However, these strong patterns at $m=\pm1\notin K$ disappear at the folding operation.
elements.sent-2411-090: To detect the target $a_{10}$, we thus need to statistically amplify the weak available pattern $\propto Y_{10 (I,\psi)$ with the coupling coefficient $|D_{10 |=|\sin b|\ll 1$, allowed by the literal bulge structure in the sky.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: To discuss the statistical amplification of the weak signals from bulge CWDBs at various Galactic latitudes $b$, we define the joint PDF \beq \bp(I,\psi,b)=\bp(I,\psi|b) R(b).

elements.sent-2411-093: As mentioned earlier, their symmetric axes do not distinguish $\vec j$ and $-\vec j$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Then, given the parity relations in Eq. (\ref{parity ), the combination ${\cal P (\vec j)+{\cal P (-\vec j)$ is insensitive to the odd $l$ patterns, including the dipole modes $l=1$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-142: In this paper, paying attention to the dipole pattern measurement, we developed a new formulation to deal with the projected bulge structure in the sky.
